title: From Control To Surrender
study: A Study In The Life Of King David
scriptureForWeek: Selected scenes from 1 Samuel showing David’s willingness to surrender the timing of his life into God's hands.
weekTheme: Boys grasp, Men trust God will work things out One of the hardest things to deal with when you are growing up is time. Time can be so challenging to make sense of in your teenage years. There are moments when you wish you were older and could enjoy all the benefits of the next stages of life and then moments when you wish you could go back in time when things were easier and carefree. A lot of guys your age won’t think deeply about time at all; it will just kind of pass them by. But learning to trust God with his timing, for what he wants and when he wants it, is a key part of becoming a man. King David knew he had to surrender the timing of his life to God. He had to trust that God would be faithful in the process to fulfill his promise. You will remember that King Saul refused to trust God's timing and it was one of the things that cost him the kingdom. He didn’t wait for God and offered a sacrifice out of fear. David didn’t want to make the same mistake. He didn’t want to rush things or act according to his own understanding. That’s why you need to burn these verses into your brain: Trust in the LORD with all your heart; do not depend on your own understanding. Seek his will in all you do, and he will show you which path to take. Don’t be impressed with your own wisdom. Instead, fear the LORD and turn away from evil. Proverbs 3:5-7 This week, you are going to see the importance of waiting on God and surrendering things into his hands. You are going to see the power of

days.title: Worth The Wait
days.scripture: ACTS 13:36 After David had done the will of God in his own generation, he died and was buried with his ancestors, and his body decayed.
days.reflection: You live in a world of instant gratification. That means we want our desires fulfilled immediately. We want what we want, and we want it now! This affects the lives of so many men today. Because men aren’t patient, they buy things on credit cards and get into tons of debt. Even though God's word teaches that we should wait to get married to have sex, many guys won’t be patient and have sex as soon as they can. This can have disastrous consequences. Many guys cheat in school and just look up answers on the internet because they don’t have the patience or take the time to learn. This can mean they pass a few tests but never really learn anything. The desire to have what we want, when we want, is making men act in ways that are selfish and stupid. Many men today have the impulse control of toddlers. God doesn’t ask us to wait because he is trying to be a spoilsport or make us miss out on stuff. God asks us to wait because he is preparing us to be able to handle what we want. If you get something good too early, you won’t be able to control or care for it properly. If a man gets power before he is ready for it, he can get prideful and start abusing people with his control. If a man starts sleeping with a woman before they are married, he can damage her heart and his character. If a man gets all the possessions he wants, he may forget about the poor and live a selfish life. Waiting for God to act in his time is a real sign of maturity and manliness. This week, you are going to see how King David learns to trust God's timing. He doesn’t grasp for power, even though he has been anointed king. He doesn’t destroy his enemies; he waits for God's wisdom on what to do. He submits his desire to get everything all at once, believing God sees the beginning from the end and knows what he is doing. We don’t have to grasp what’s in his hand; we can wait for him to give it to us. Learning to surrender your timeline into God's hands is one of the manliest things you can do. Isaiah 64:4-5 reminds us of this: Since ancient times no one has heard, no ear has perceived, no eye has seen any God besides you, who acts on behalf of those who wait for him. You come to the help of those who gladly do right, who remember your ways. David learned to trust God in the process. Not just the promise. He knew that the challenges he was going through were helping him become a king who would honor God and serve well. Not a King like Saul who would take things into his own hands with disastrous consequences. If you can learn to trust God with his timing as you are growing as a man, you are going to have a massive advantage over other guys around you. You will be kept safe from stupidity and prepared with wisdom. You will know when to do what in order to be successful in life and pleasing to God. Surrender your agenda and trust God with his timing. Today's verse shows us that David had all the time he needed to fulfill God's purpose for his life. And you do too. You don’t have to grasp, freak out, or rush ahead out of fear of missing out. God will make all things beautiful for your life in his time.
questions: Can you think of a couple of ways that the world tries to manipulate guys your age into instant gratification? Why can this be so dangerous?

Are there any areas in your life you feel tempted with instant gratification? Would you be willing to share what they are? What do you think will happen if you don’t learn to become a man who can wait for God's timing?

Ask the person you are doing this study with to share a time they surrendered their timing and trusted God’s and he came through for them. Where were they tempted to act before God said so, and why was it worth the wait?

thoughts.verse: I remain confident of this: I will see the goodness of the LORD in the land of the living. Wait for the LORD; be strong and take heart and wait for the LORD.
prompts: Is there something that stands out to you in it?
Is there a question you have about any of the words and what they mean?
Do you think you could explain what it means to someone else if they asked?

days.prayer: Take a moment to ask God for patience and perspective in any area you feel impatient in right now.

days.scripture: 1 SAMUEL 24:1-22 After Saul returned from fighting the Philistines, he was told that David had gone into the wilderness of En- gedi. So Saul chose 3,000 elite troops from all Israel and went to search for David and his men near the rocks of the wild goats. At the place where the road passes some sheepfolds, Saul went into a cave to relieve himself. But as it happened, David and his men were hiding farther back in that very cave! “Now’s your opportunity!” David’s men whispered to him. “Today the LORD is telling you, ‘I will certainly put your enemy into your power, to do with as you wish.’” So David crept forward and cut off a piece of the hem of Saul’s robe. But then David’s conscience began bothering him because he had cut Saul’s robe. He said to his men, “The LORD forbid that I should do this to my lord the king. I shouldn’t attack the LORD’s anointed one, for the LORD himself has chosen him.” So David restrained his men and did not let them kill Saul. After Saul had left the cave and gone on his way, David came out and shouted after him, “My lord the king!” And when Saul looked around, David bowed low before him. Then he shouted to Saul, “Why do you listen to the people who say I am trying to harm you? This very day you can see with your own eyes it isn’t true. For the LORD placed you at my mercy back there in the cave. Some of my men told me to kill you, but I spared you. For I said, ‘I will never harm the king—he is the LORD’s anointed one.’ Look, my father, at what I have in my hand. It is a piece of the hem of your robe! I cut it off, but I didn’t kill you. This proves that I am not trying to harm you and that I have not sinned against you, even though you have been hunting for me to kill me. “May the LORD judge between us. Perhaps the LORD will punish you for what you are trying to do to me, but I will never harm you. As that old proverb says, ‘From evil people come evil deeds.’ So you can be sure I will never harm you. Who is the king of Israel trying to catch anyway? Should he spend his time chasing one who is as worthless as a dead dog or a single flea? May the LORD therefore judge which of us is right and punish the guilty one. He is my advocate, and he will rescue me from your power!” When David had finished speaking, Saul called back, “Is that really you, my son David?” Then he began to cry. And he said to David, “You are a better man than I am, for you have repaid me good for evil. Yes, you have been amazingly kind to me today, for when the LORD put me in a place where you could have killed me, you didn’t do it. Who else would let his enemy get away when he had him in his power? May the LORD reward you well for the kindness you have shown me today. And now I realize that you are surely going to be king, and that the kingdom of Israel will flourish under your rule. Now swear to me by the LORD that when that happens you will not kill my family and destroy my line of descendants!” So David promised this to Saul with an oath. Then Saul went home, but David and his men went back to their stronghold.
days.reflection: Discernment is one of the most important things a guy your age needs to develop. But it's also something guys your age rarely think about. When was the last time you heard one of your friends say, “I can’t hang out today because I need to take some time to grow in discernment”? That never happens. ☺ But maybe it should. Discernment means: “The act or process of exhibiting keen insight and good judgment” Without discernment, you will have bad perspective and bad judgment. This can lead you to make foolish decisions with bad consequences. The reason you need to learn about discernment is because there will be so many voices telling you what to do in your life. Discernment helps you become skillful and wise in navigating the complexities of your life. It helps you answer questions like… Whose opinion should I listen to in this situation? Is this situation a test to pass or a temptation to avoid? Is this God's will or my will? What will the long-term consequences be if I do this? David had discernment, and he shows it in today's reading. Saul has 3000 of his best men out looking for David. And in what can only be described as one of the weirdest scenes in the bible, Saul comes into the very cave David and his men are hiding in to take a dump. What a crazy scene, and what a wild situation David finds himself in. David’s enemy is in front of him. For all intents and purposes, it looks like God has delivered him into David’s hands. David has to discern what to do. David’s men urged him on. "Now’s your opportunity!” David’s men whispered to him. “Today the LORD is telling you, ‘I will certainly put your enemy into your power, to do with as you wish.’” So David crept forward and cut off a piece of the hem of Saul’s robe. David’s men assumed they knew God's will. They looked at the circumstances without trying to discern all that could be happening. They assumed this is what God was doing. They rushed to judgment without a process of discernment. But David paused to listen to his heart. And he came to a different conclusion. But then David’s conscience began bothering him because he had cut Saul’s robe. He said to his men, “The LORD forbid that I should do this to my lord the king. I shouldn’t attack the LORD’s anointed one, for the LORD himself has chosen him.” So David restrained his men and did not let them kill Saul. David restrained his men. David discerned something different. Even though he knew he was meant to be king, he didn’t want to become king like this. He wanted God's will, but he wanted it God's way. He was willing to wait for God to act, rather than just taking things into his own hands. As you go through your life, you will have to become a wise and discerning man. You will have to learn to listen to the Lord and not just the voices around you. This can be really hard at times because others may look at things from a worldly perspective without taking God's word and will into account. Learn to submit every challenging situation to God. Learn to listen to your conscience. Learn to ask God before you act. It will make you into a man that God can trust and others respect. Surrender each situation to God and the path will become clear.
questions: Can you think of someone in your life who is really discerning? How can you tell? How do they do it?
What are one or two things you could do to become more discerning as a young man right now? Is there something you are struggling with right now that you could use discernment in?

Ask the person you are doing this study with to share how they learned to become discerning when they were your age. What are some of the decisions or choices that caused them to want to become more discerning?

thoughts.verse: Don’t copy the behavior and customs of this world, but let God transform you into a new person by changing the way you think. Then you will learn to know God’s will for you, which is good and pleasing and perfect. Romans 12:2 Read through his verse slowly a second time. Is there something that stands out to you in it? Is there a question you have about any of the words and what they mean? Do you think you could explain what it means to someone else if they asked?

days.prayer: Take a moment to ask God to give you a heart of discernment. Ask him for wisdom and perspective on some steps you can take to grow in this area.

days.title: Belief Vs Faith
days.scripture: 1 SAMUEL 26:1-25 Now some men from Ziph came to Saul at Gibeah to tell him, “David is hiding on the hill of Hakilah, which overlooks Jeshimon.” So Saul took 3,000 of Israel’s elite troops and went to hunt him down in the wilderness of Ziph. Saul camped along the road beside the hill of Hakilah, near Jeshimon, where David was hiding. When David learned that Saul had come after him into the wilderness, he sent out spies to verify the report of Saul’s arrival. David slipped over to Saul’s camp one night to look around. Saul and Abner son of Ner, the commander of his army, were sleeping inside a ring formed by the slumbering warriors.“Who will volunteer to go in there with me?” David asked Ahimelech the Hittite and Abishai son of Zeruiah, Joab’s brother. “I’ll go with you,” replied. So David and Abishai went right into Saul’s camp and found him asleep, with his spear stuck in the ground beside his head. Abner and the soldiers were lying asleep around him. “God has surely handed your enemy over to you this time!” Abishai whispered to David. “Let me pin him to the ground with one thrust of the spear; I won’t need to strike twice!” “No!” David said. “Don’t kill him. For who can remain innocent after attacking the LORD’s anointed one? Surely the LORD will strike Saul down someday, or he will die of old age or in battle. The LORD forbid that I should kill the one he has anointed! But take his spear and that jug of water beside his head, and then let’s get out of here!” So David took the spear and jug of water that were near Saul’s head. Then he and Abishai got away without anyone seeing them or even waking up, because the LORD had put Saul’s men into a deep sleep. David climbed the hill opposite the camp until he was at a safe distance. Then he shouted down to the soldiers and to Abner son of Ner, “Wake up, Abner!” “Who is it?” Abner demanded. “Well, Abner, you’re a great man, aren’t you?” David taunted. “Where in all Israel is there anyone as mighty? So why haven’t you guarded your master the king when someone came to kill him? This isn’t good at all! I swear by the LORD that you and your men deserve to die, because you failed to protect your master, the LORD’s anointed! Look around! Where are the king’s spear and the jug of water that were beside his head?” Saul recognized David’s voice and called out, “Is that you, my son David?” And David replied, “Yes, my lord the king. Why are you chasing me? What have I done? What is my crime? But now let my lord the king listen to his servant. If the LORD has stirred you up against me, then let him accept my offering. But if this is simply a human scheme, then may those involved be cursed by the LORD. For they have driven me from my home, so I can no longer live among the LORD’s people, and they have said, ‘Go, worship pagan gods.’ Must I die on foreign soil, far from the presence of the LORD? Why has the king of Israel come out to search for a single flea? Why does he hunt me down like a partridge on the mountains?” Then Saul confessed, “I have sinned. Come back home, my son, and I will no longer try to harm you, for you valued my life today. I have been a fool and very, very wrong.” “Here is your spear, O king,” David replied. “Let one of your young men come over and get it. The LORD gives his own reward for doing good and for being loyal, and I refused to kill you even when the LORD placed you in my power, for you are the LORD’s anointed one. Now may the LORD value my life, even as I have valued yours today. May he rescue me from all my troubles.” And Saul said to David, “Blessings on you, my son David. You will do many heroic deeds, and you will surely succeed.” Then David went away, and Saul returned home.
days.reflection: To be a godly man, you have to learn to live by faith. But that can be a confusing idea. Does faith mean you believe certain ideas about God? Does it mean you think the bible is true, that there is a God, and that you go somewhere when you die? Faith does include believing the right things, but it's more than that. Lots of people can believe the right thing but still not live by faith. The Bible says in James 2:19-20: “You say you have faith, for you believe that there is one God. Good for you! Even the demons believe this, and they tremble in terror. How foolish! Can’t you see that faith without good deeds is useless?” Faith is more than knowing the right things. Faith is acting on them as if they are true. Faith is trust. One classic illustration of faith vs. belief is about a parachute on a plane. Imagine you are on a plane and the engines have failed. The flight crew hands you a parachute and tells you to strap it on and jump out; if you don’t, you will die. You can believe the parachute will hold you up. You can believe in gravity and the principles that help the parachute work. But you don’t really believe until you put the parachute on, and jump out of the plane. You believe when you entrust yourself to the parachute to help you land safely. You can crash and die believing in the parachute while never putting it on. In the same way, you have to trust that God will work out his plan, not just believe there is one. It’s about real-world surrender, not just having the right ideas in your head. Today, David shows you what real faith looks like in the real world. Saul is out on the hunt for David again. He is paranoid about David and won't rest until he is dead. Once again, David has the chance to kill Saul and be done with this bullying and harassment forever. He could step right onto the throne and just get on with his life. Saul is in a deep sleep, and David has him right where he wants him. His servant Abishai fails to discern what God is actually doing and urges David to kill Saul. But David trusts God and has decided in his heart to surrender his kingship into God's hands. Look at what he says. “Surely the LORD will strike Saul down someday, or he will die of old age or in battle. The LORD forbid that I should kill the one he has anointed! David trusted in the Lord. David knew that “surely” God would remove Saul when the timing was right. He was willing to trust God with his destiny, rather than take it for himself. This showed David’s authentic faith, real trust, and patience with God's process for his life. David didn’t have to act, because he knew the Lord would act. God wants you to have real faith. To learn to trust him, not just believe ideas about him. That’s why it’s so cool that you are taking the time to learn about him and grow as a man here on the Primal Path. Looking at the lives of men in history and seeing how God came through for them will build your confidence that God will come through for you. When the time comes, put the parachute on and jump. God will always reward a man who lives by faith.
questions: What is the difference between David’s faith in this passage and the demon’s faith in James 2?
Why do you think it’s easier to “believe” in God than have “faith” in God?
Ask the person you are doing this study with to share one of the first times they really lived and acted in faith, not just “belief.” What was it, what happened, and what did they learn?

thoughts.verse: And it is impossible to please God without faith. Anyone who wants to come to him must believe that God exists and that he rewards those who sincerely seek him. Hebrews 11:6 Read through his verse slowly a second time. Is there something that stands out to you in it? Is there a question you have about any of the words and what they mean? Do you think you could explain what it means to someone else if they asked?

days.prayer: Take a moment to ask God to increase your faith. Ask him to help you learn to trust him in your daily life.

days.title: Letting God Sort It Out
days.scripture: 2 SAMUEL 16:5-10 As King David came to Bahurim, a man came out of the village cursing them. It was Shimei son of Gera, from the same clan as Saul’s family. He threw stones at the king and the king’s officers and all the mighty warriors who surrounded him.“Get out of here, you murderer, you scoundrel!” he shouted at David. “The LORD is paying you back for all the bloodshed in Saul’s clan. You stole his throne, and now the LORD has given it to your son Absalom. At last you will taste some of your own medicine, for you are a murderer!” “Why should this dead dog curse my lord the king?” Abishai son of Zeruiah demanded. “Let me go over and cut off his head!” “No!” the king said. “Who asked your opinion, you sons of Zeruiah! If the LORD has told him to curse me, who are you to stop him?”
days.reflection: When someone insults us, it hurts. Our natural reaction is to fight back and trade insult for insult. We can feel the need to defend ourselves and clear our own name. We often feel like it’s up to us to protect our reputation in the world. This can happen in real life or when people talk smack online or on social media. How should a wise young man respond when this happens? In today’s reading, you see something amazing in the life of David: his willingness to surrender his reputation to God. David is in a hard season in his life. It seems like everything is going against him. In this scene, David is having to flee the city because one of his sons has stolen the throne from him and turned his back on him. To make matters worse, as he is leaving the city, one of Saul’s relatives comes out and starts cursing David. He throws rocks at him and mocks him in his distress. He even says that it's God paying him back for what he did to Saul. In today’s world, everyone thinks they can insult anyone they want, but insulting a king back then was a major offense. It could cost you your life. David’s men freak out and demand that he acts to stop the insults. Abishai wants to go over and cut Shimei’s head off. But look at how David responds. “No!” the king said. “Who asked your opinion, you sons of Zeruiah! If the LORD has told him to curse me, who are you to stop him?” David didn’t want to take things into his own hands. David knew that in due time God would take care of his name. David was willing to surrender his reputation to God because he trusted God's promise that he would be restored to the throne and that he would always have one of his descends ruling after him. His faith in God enabled him to overlook these insults because his future was secure. Romans 12:17-19 says something similar to us. Never pay back evil with more evil. Do things in such a way that everyone can see you are honorable. Do all that you can to live in peace with everyone. Dear friends, never take revenge. Leave that to the righteous anger of God. For the Scriptures say, “I will take revenge; I will pay them back,” says the LORD. As you go through life, you are going to have people who don’t like you. You may even have people slander you and seek to sabotage you. You need to discern what God would have you do in moments like these. Defending your reputation may end up leading to more drama, and escalating the situation. Learning to walk away, trust God, and get on with your life is often the best thing to do. God will make things right in his time. If you walk with integrity, it will eventually come to light. God has an amazing way of making sure that happens. Take heart in this promise from Psalm 37:5-7. Commit everything you do to the LORD. Trust him, and he will help you. He will make your innocence radiate like the dawn, and the justice of your cause will shine like the noonday sun. Be still in the presence of the LORD, and wait patiently for him to act. Don’t worry about evil people who prosper or fret about their wicked schemes. Take heart. God will sort it out in the end. (To see the full story of what happened to Shimei, look up the details of his life and how it ended for him.)
questions: Has anyone ever insulted you or picked on you? What happened and how did it make you feel?
Do you think it's realistic to trust your reputation to God at your age? Why or why not?

Ask the person you are doing this study with to share a time they refused to respond when being attacked and trusted God to work it out. How did God give them the restraint not to respond, and how did God work it out?

thoughts.verse: Read through these verses slowly. God blesses those who are persecuted for doing right, for the Kingdom of Heaven is theirs. “God blesses you when people mock you and persecute you and lie about you and say all sorts of evil things against you because you are my followers. Be happy about it! Be very glad! For a great reward awaits you in heaven. And remember, the ancient prophets were persecuted in the same way.
prompts: Is there something that stands out to you in any of them?
Is there a question you have about any of the words and what they mean?
Do you think you could explain what it means to someone else if they asked?

days.prayer: Take a moment to ask God for the grace to surrender your reputation to him. Ask him to bless anyone who is hassling you or picking on you right now.

days.title: In His Time
days.scripture: 2 SAMUEL 5:1-10 Then all the tribes of Israel went to David at Hebron and told him, “We are your own flesh and blood. In the past, when Saul was our king, you were the one who really led the forces of Israel. And the LORD told you, ‘You will be the shepherd of my people Israel. You will be Israel’s leader.’” So there at Hebron, King David made a covenant before the LORD with all the elders of Israel. And they anointed him king of Israel. David was thirty years old when he began to reign, and he reigned forty years in all. He had reigned over Judah from Hebron for seven years and six months, and from Jerusalem he reigned over all Israel and Judah for thirty-three years. David then led his men to Jerusalem to fight against the Jebusites, the original inhabitants of the land who were living there. The Jebusites taunted David, saying, “You’ll never get in here! Even the blind and lame could keep you out!” For the Jebusites thought they were safe. But David captured the fortress of Zion, which is now called the City of David. On the day of the attack, David said to his troops, “I hate those ‘lame’ and ‘blind’ Jebusites. Whoever attacks them should strike by going into the city through the water tunnel.” That is the origin of the saying, “The blind and the lame may not enter the house.” So David made the fortress his home, and he called it the City of David. He extended the city, starting at the supporting terraces and working inward. And David became more and more powerful, because the LORD God of Heaven’s Armies was with him.
days.reflection: Learning to surrender to God and trust him with the timing of your life is at the heart of what makes a man. Grasping for control is what boys do. When you scramble to remain in control of your life, you have to rely on yourself. You are limited by your own ability to hustle and perform to try and get what you want. It can be exhausting trying to fix everything yourself, with no guarantees that what you want to happen will come to pass. Being a control freak never works out. Look at what happened to King Saul. Saul ended up dying a terrible death. He was badly injured by the Philistines and ended up falling on his own sword, committing suicide. What David predicted came true. He didn’t have to take things into his own hands and kill Saul to see God fulfill his promise. When the people heard of Saul's death, they rushed to David and made him their king. But I want you to notice something in this passage. It seems like a small detail, but it's important to pay attention to. “David was thirty years old when he began to reign.” David was a teenager when he was first anointed as king. Yet it has taken well over a decade for him to actually ascend the throne. He had several opportunities to take the throne into his own hands, but instead, he was willing to wait for God to bring it to pass. And now, at 30, his rule and reign begin. The first thing he does as king is capture the city of Jerusalem from the Jebusites. This is still the same Jerusalem that people take holy land tours to see today. He established and expanded the city as the capital of the kingdom of heaven on earth. When he gained power, he used it for God's glory and the good of his people. His training and preparation had made him into a man God could trust with power, who would care for the people and would do God's will (with a few bad moments we will talk about soon). As you make your way through the next years from adolescence into manhood, you are going to have to make a choice. Will you trust yourself and do things when you want to? Or will you trust God and wait on his timing? These decisions are going to come at you fast. Will you trust God's timing with your school? Will you do the right thing, at the right time, in the right way? Will you trust God with the girls you date? Not pushing physical boundaries beyond what God has said. Believing he knows best about sex and dating because he created it. Will you trust God with your money? Learning as a teenager to honor him with tithing, stewardship, and generosity? Will you trust him with your college choices? Seeking his will and guidance for the years ahead. If you do, you will walk in the path of life and enjoy his favor and blessing, If you scramble, who knows how it will turn out. Learn from David, and learn from Saul. And remember this promise that David wrote from his own experience of relying on God. Delight yourself in the LORD, and He will give you the desires of your heart. Commit your way to the LORD; trust in Him, and He will do it. He will bring forth your righteousness like the dawn, your justice like the noonday sun... Psalm 37:4-6 Choose to surrender now, and God will help you in the days ahead.
questions: Of all the lessons David learned about trusting God with the timing of his life, which one stands out to you the most? Why?
What are a couple of areas you feel a bit of a “control freak” tendency in right now?
Ask the person you are doing this study with to share what stood out to them about David and Saul’s responses to the Lord. What did they learn about surrendering to God's timing this week?

thoughts.verse: Read through these verses slowly. The LORD directs the steps of the godly. He delights in every detail of their lives. Though they stumble, they will never fall, for the LORD holds them by the hand.
prompts: Is there something that stands out to you in any of them?
Is there a question you have about any of the words and what they mean?
Do you think you could explain what it means to someone else if they asked?

days.prayer: Take a moment to ask God to help you trust him and his timing. Ask God to help you surrender anything you are trying to hold onto or maintain control over and thank him for his faithfulness.

extras: WEEK 6 MANSCHOOL DINNER MAN SCHOOL REMINDER The recipe for fun is pretty simple when you’re raising boys: add to any activity an element of danger, stir in a little exploration, add a dash of destruction, and you’ve got yourself a winner. - John Eldredge OPTION 1. ACTIVITY: DINNER It's one thing to cook a steak; another thing to prepare a meal and get the timing right on all the dishes to be hot and ready at once. Pick a meal to cook together with your son that requires 3 or 4 dishes being prepared and served together. Cook a dessert as well. Let him see how important it is to get the timing right in order for things to come together. OPTION 2. WATCH AMAZING GRACE An incredible story of William Wilberforce and his patience and trust in God's timing to stop the slave trade in England. Wilberforce was a man of faith, patience, and deep surrender to the Lord. An epic true story of patience leading to breakthrough. https://youtu.be/Q6Cv5P9H9qU OPTION 3. DINNER AND DISCUSSION Take your son out to one of his favorite restaurants or for desserts and talk through what it means to honor God's process and timing in his life. This is a good chance to talk through process and responsibility. How do you know when you are ready for something? Where are you tempted to take timing into your own hands? What wisdom can you see in making sure you are ready for something? How have you rushed things before and it hasn’t worked out? How can you be patient and not fall into the same traps that other guys your age fall into?
FIELD NOTES PRAYER FOR MY SON
